text:
Admission Date:  1979-02-15              Discharge Date:   1973-03-19

Date of Birth:  1976-09-25             Sex:   F

Service: UROLOGY

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Kimberly
Chief Complaint:
Left renal mass with renal vein inferior vena cava thrombus just
beneath the level of the hepatic veins, a level III thrombus.


Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Dr. Williams PROCEDURES PERFORMED: LEFT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY and
right renal exposure with exposure of the right renal hilum,
isolation of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava, and
inferior vena caval thrombectomy

Dr. Amanda Summers PROCEDURES PERFORMED:  Exploratory laparotomy,
mobilization of the liver, and takedown of the short caudate
branches and vena cava thrombectomy.


History of Present Illness:
65 year old female with renal mass extending into
IVC s/p open left radical nephrectomy and IVC thrombectomy. She
was recently discharged from Palmetto Health Baptist Parkridge (05-16 through
02-20)and per chart review "for dyspnea and fatigue" for 1 week.
She was found to have an elevated D dimer and was sent to the
Center For Behavioral Medicine ED for evaluation. A CTA was performed to rule out
PE (which was negative), but a L renal mass suspicious for RCC
was incidentally noted with thrombus extending into the IVC.

                            **FINAL REPORT 2015-03-05**

   MRSA SCREEN (Final 2015-03-05):    No MRSA isolated.


Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. Johnston was admitted to Urology after undergoing  Exploratory
laparotomy, mobilization of the liver, and takedown of the short
caudate branches and
vena cava thrombectomy and open radical left nephrectomy. No
concerning intra-operative events occurred; please see dictated
operative note for details. The patient received perioperative
antibiotic prophylaxis. The patient was transferred to the ICU
from the PACU in stable condition.  On POD2 she was transferred
to the  general surgical floor on the East United Memorial Medical Center North Street Campus campus where
she remained until discharged. She was hydrated for urine output
>30cc/hour, provided with pneumoboots and incentive spirometry
for prophylaxis, and ambulated once. She received one unit of
blood while on the floor and was kept on continous monitoring
throughout her stay. Pain control was transitioned from epidural
and PCA to oral analgesics, diet was advanced from clears to
regular diet. Urethral catheter (Foley) was removed without
difficulty. The remainder of the hospital course was relatively
unremarkable. The patient was discharged in stable condition,
eating well, ambulating independently, voiding without
difficulty, and with pain controlled on oral analgesics. On
exam, incision was clean, dry, and intact, with no evidence of
hematoma collection or infection. The patient was given explicit
instructions to follow-up in clinic with Dr. Williams.

Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left renal mass with renal vein inferior vena cava thrombus just
beneath the level of the hepatic veins, a level III thrombus.

PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:  Renal cell carcinoma with tumor
thrombus.


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
-Resume your pre-admission/home medications except as noted.
ALWAYS call to inform, review and discuss any medication changes
and your post-operative course with your primary care doctor. Do
NOT take Aspirin/aspirin containing products unless advised to
do so by your doctors.

-You will be discharged home on LOVENOX, one of your
pre-admission medications. Please continue this medication until
otherwise advised to stop by your PCP or Dr. Williams.

-Do not lift anything heavier than a phone book (10 pounds) or
drive until you are seen by your Urologist in follow-up

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication.
Discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a
stool softener, NOT a laxative

-Do not eat constipating foods for 2-4 weeks, drink plenty of
fluids to keep hydrated

-No vigorous physical activity or sports for 4 weeks or until
otherwise advised

-Tylenol should be your first line pain medication, a narcotic
pain medication has been prescribed for breakthrough pain >4.
Replace Tylenol with narcotic pain medication.

-Max daily Tylenol (acetaminophen) dose is 4 grams from ALL
sources, note that narcotic pain medication also contains
Tylenol

-You may shower normally but do NOT immerse your incisions or
bathe

-Do not drive or drink alcohol while taking narcotics and do not
operate dangerous machinery.

-You may shower but do not bathe, swim or immerse your incision.

-Call your Urologist's office today to schedule/confirm your
follow-up appointment in 3 weeks AND if you have any questions.


Followup Instructions:
Call your Urologist's office today to schedule/confirm your
follow-up appointment AND if you have any questions.
